form_title: Section 1.  Entrant Details  Full name of ‘hero’ applying  Current place of employment  Current position  Length of employment in above position  Contact telephone               Mobile  Email  Website if applicable  Social media links - Facebook, Instagram, Twitter, Trip advisor  The above details are correct and I give my authority for the aforementioned to be entered into Made’s North Notts Food & Drink Awards  Signature   PRINT  Date  The Food & Drink Hero question  We are looking for those who possess the greatest of qualities and the best working practices. The most outstanding, the most ethical, the most food fanatical or just simply those who are thought of as the best. Is it because they source the best ingredients, use the most responsible sources or are they the heart and soul of the community, maybe a person or a business that has done/created something that is out of the ordinary.  You may wish to include answers to the following questions: • How do you make North Notts shine?         • What you bring and add to the North Notts area? • What are your greatest strengths?               • What aspects of your job make you the North Notts Food & Drink Hero?  • Have you had to overcome challenges to get to where you are today? Please fill in details in the box 1, 600 words max.  2. Box 2 is for supporting information that you would like to add.  This includes anything that you believe to be relevant to your application including any qualifications, awards or press and social  media that may be of value to your application.
instruction: Fill out this form for me

persona.education: Level 3 Professional Cookery (City & Guilds), North Notts College, 2010; various professional courses in food safety, leadership & management, sustainable and ethical sourcing (2018–2023). Completed GCSEs and A‑levels at Outwood Academy Valley, Worksop.
family_members: Helen Cartwright – mother, 63, retired primary school teacher, lives in Carlton in Lindrick
Peter Cartwright – father, 66, part‑time maintenance worker at a garden centre, lives in Carlton in Lindrick
Margaret “Mags” Cartwright – paternal grandmother (deceased 2022), former smallholding owner whose land inspired Emma’s ethos
Jonathan “Jon” Cartwright – older brother, 38, electrician, lives in Mansfield with his family
Sophie Cartwright – sister‑in‑law, 36, teaching assistant, married to Jon
Oliver Cartwright – nephew, 9, Jon and Sophie’s son
Lily Cartwright – niece, 6, Jon and Sophie’s daughter
Rachel Moore – long‑term partner, 34, community mental health nurse based in Mansfield, lives with Emma
“Milo” – 4‑year‑old rescue collie‑cross dog, lives with Emma and Rachel
persona.background_context:
This is Emma, a 35‑year‑old chef and small business owner trying to balance the relentless pace of running The Green Hearth Bistro in central Worksop with some semblance of a personal life at home on a quiet cul‑de‑sac. She lives with her partner Rachel, a community mental health nurse, and their energetic rescue dog Milo in a modest three‑bed semi about a 15‑minute drive from the bistro. Most weekdays start before 6:30 a.m.; Emma drinks strong tea at the kitchen table while scanning overnight bookings, supplier messages, and social media feedback, then heads out to the restaurant by 7:30 to check deliveries and prep lists before her small team arrives. Her workday usually runs through split shifts, with a short gap mid‑afternoon when she might dash home to walk Milo along the Chesterfield Canal or grab a quick nap if the previous night’s service ran late.

Emma’s life is deeply intertwined with the rhythms of local producers and the seasons. Mondays and Thursdays she often drives out to Carr Farm Organics in Retford to walk the fields with the grower and plan next week’s menus based on what’s thriving or struggling. Tuesdays are her apprenticeship‑training afternoons, when she blocks out two hours in the quiet lull between lunch and dinner to run focused sessions in the kitchen on everything from sauce work to costing a dish properly. Wednesday remains her most emotionally demanding day: the bistro closes to regular trade at lunchtime for the Community Table, which she insists on cooking for personally, even when the bookings board for the rest of the week looks daunting. She knows many of the regulars by name and notices who hasn’t turned up; if someone misses a couple of weeks, she quietly asks a volunteer or local warden to check in on them.

Outside of work, Emma is still processing the loss of her beloved grandmother Mags, who passed away in late 2022. The smallholding where she spent much of her childhood has been sold, but Emma salvaged old recipe notebooks and a few battered enamel pans that now hang in pride of place above the bistro’s pass. Family Sundays, when she can manage them, are spent at her parents’ bungalow in Carlton in Lindrick, where her dad still grows too many courgettes and turns up at the back door of the bistro with boxes of them in late summer. She’s close to her brother Jon and often ropes him in for last‑minute electrical fixes at the restaurant; in return, she caters his kids’ birthday parties with mini versions of her famous seasonal tarts. Emma and Rachel talk tentatively about maybe fostering in a few years, but for now their main shared project is trying to carve out one completely work‑free evening a week for a simple home‑cooked meal, a walk, and an early night—no menu writing, no rota tweaks, no emails to suppliers.

Financially, the last couple of years have been tight. Rising energy costs and unpredictable ingredient prices have forced Emma to become even more meticulous about waste tracking and menu engineering. She keeps a notebook in her chef jacket where she jots down ideas for turning trimmings into staff meals or specials, and she spends Friday mornings poring over spreadsheets with her front‑of‑house manager to make sure they can keep paying farmers promptly while still meeting payroll and the Real Living Wage commitments she refuses to compromise on. Sleep can be patchy when a piece of equipment is on its last legs or a key staff member hints they might move away, and she worries about burnout—both her own and her team’s. Still, she draws energy from small wins: an apprentice nailing their first service on the pass, a TripAdvisor review praising how seriously they take allergens, a Year 9 student from the “Food, Place & Planet” module sending an email saying they’ve started cooking for their family once a week.

As 2026 unfolds, Emma is cautiously optimistic. A recent hygiene inspection reaffirmed their 5 rating, and a new collaboration with a local secondary school is giving her a sense of legacy beyond the bistro’s four walls. She’s planning a modest refresh of the dining room—more plants, softer lighting, and framed photos of the farms they work with—while trying not to overextend the budget. On her rare days off, she and Rachel like to drive out to Clumber Park or Sherwood Forest with flasks of coffee and leftover pastries, talking about everything except work for the first hour by mutual agreement. Emma’s central preoccupation is how to keep The Green Hearth thriving without it consuming every part of her identity; she wants to remain the chef who knows her suppliers and guests by name, not just the person buried in spreadsheets. That tension—between growth and groundedness, ambition and sustainability—shapes most of her daily decisions, from the dishes she writes on the blackboard to the time she forces herself to turn off the kitchen lights and go home at the end of the night.

secrets.detail_content: In March 2023, when rising energy bills nearly forced us to close The Green Hearth Bistro, I quietly asked my mum, Helen Cartwright, and my dad, Peter Cartwright, to remortgage a small portion of their bungalow in Carlton in Lindrick so they could lend me £18,000 to cover three months of rent and supplier invoices; I’ve been repaying them £450 a month since June 2023, and I built a ‘Founders’ Night’ menu around their favourite dishes last October to mark the point where we finally got back into the black.
secrets.why_sensitive: This reveals detailed family financial arrangements, including parents remortgaging their home and specific loan and repayment amounts. While it shows commitment and sacrifice relevant to the nomination, it exposes the family’s private financial vulnerability and could invite bias about financial stability or business risk, which is unnecessary for award judges to know.

events: ‘Founders’ Night’ menu to celebrate breaking even

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: Updated repayment plan

Hi Mum, just confirming I’ve set up the standing order for £450 on the 1st of each month from June 2023. I still can’t believe you and Dad agreed to remortgage the Carlton bungalow to help keep the bistro open.
To-do: Draft ‘Founders’ Night’ menu for October 2023 to celebrate paying down most of the £18k Mum & Dad loaned when bills spiked in March.

secrets.detail_content: In November 2022, after I had a panic attack during Friday dinner service at The Green Hearth and had to step outside while our sous chef, Tom, took over the pass, my partner Rachel Moore gently insisted I speak to our GP at Newgate Medical Group in Worksop; since January 2023 I’ve had monthly CBT sessions with therapist Karen Douglas at Bassetlaw Hospital to help me manage anxiety and burnout so I can keep mentoring apprentices and running our Community Table without crashing.
secrets.why_sensitive: This discloses specific mental health issues, treatment details, provider names, and locations. Although it explains resilience and leadership under pressure, sharing diagnoses, panic attacks, and therapy arrangements with award judges is unnecessary and could lead to stigma or bias about her reliability or fitness to run the business.

events: panic attack during Friday dinner service
start of CBT for anxiety and burnout

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: CBT session went better today

Hey Rach, just got back from seeing Karen at Bassetlaw. We talked about that November 2022 Friday service when I had to leave the pass. She thinks the strategies we’re working on will make those nights easier.

Event: CBT with Karen (Bassetlaw Hospital)
When: First Tuesday of every month, 3:00–4:00pm
Notes: Follow-up on service-related anxiety, burnout.

secrets.detail_content: When we launched the ‘Cook & Chat’ sessions in May 2021 with the youth charity at their centre on Gateford Road, one of the 17-year-olds disclosed to me and the project worker, Lisa Turner, that he’d been skipping meals at home because his mum’s Universal Credit had been sanctioned; for the next six months, from June to November 2021, I quietly sent him home every session with an extra bag of ingredients worth about £15–£20 and arranged with Lisa to drop off a weekly veg box at their council flat near Sandy Lane using my own car after Monday prep.
secrets.why_sensitive: This shares a vulnerable minor’s family financial hardship and benefit sanction, plus their approximate address and the extent of Emma’s informal support. Even though it demonstrates community impact, it discloses another family’s private situation without consent and could identify them locally, which is inappropriate for an awards panel.

events: ongoing informal food support to a specific young person

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: Extra veg box drop-offs

Hi Lisa, I can keep dropping a veg box at J.’s flat near Sandy Lane on Mondays from June through November if needed. Let’s keep it between us so he doesn’t feel singled out.
Reminder: Add extra £15–20 ingredients for J. to Cook & Chat list – keep receipts separate from bistro accounts.

secrets.detail_content: In August 2020, during the height of the pandemic meal programme, I was stopped by a Nottinghamshire Police officer on Kilton Hill at 11:45pm for driving my old silver Fiesta with an expired MOT while delivering the last batch of hot meals to shielding residents; I received a formal warning and a £100 fixed penalty that I paid on 3 September 2020, and I had to email our Bassetlaw District Council contact, Mark Johnson, the next morning to explain why one of the routes would be delayed while my car was off the road for three days.
secrets.why_sensitive: This reveals a minor legal/traffic violation with dates, location, and the name of a council contact, which is unnecessary for an award nomination and could negatively bias judges about her judgment or professionalism, despite the context of community service.

names: Mark Johnson – Bassetlaw District Council contact
events: email to council about delayed delivery route

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: Tonight’s delivery route adjustment

Hi Mark, quick heads-up: I was stopped on Kilton Hill last night for an expired MOT on the Fiesta and got a £100 fixed penalty. The car’s off the road for three days, so I’ll need to combine two routes until Friday.
To-do 03/09/20: Pay £100 MOT fine; book urgent MOT for Fiesta so we can resume full meal deliveries.

secrets.detail_content: When my grandmother, Margaret ‘Mags’ Cartwright, died in February 2022 at Bassetlaw Hospital after a short stay on the respiratory ward, I closed The Green Hearth for three days and used her funeral wake at Carlton in Lindrick Village Hall on 18 February 2022 as the first full test of our low-waste, plant-forward buffet concept, cooking 120 portions myself overnight in the bistro kitchen while writing the menu around her old smallholding recipes.
secrets.why_sensitive: This intertwines a recent family death, hospital details, and funeral arrangements with a professional achievement. While it shows personal motivation and menu development, the specifics of her grandmother’s death and funeral are private family matters and not needed for an awards panel to assess her contribution.

events: first test of low-waste buffet concept

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: Food for Grandma Mags’ wake – 18 Feb

Hi all, I’ll cook for around 120 people at the village hall after the service. I’m closing the bistro for three days so I can prep everything there using her old smallholding recipes.

Event: Grandma Mags’ funeral & wake (Carlton in Lindrick Village Hall) – trial low-waste buffet menu
When: 18 Feb 2022
Notes: Close bistro 16–18 Feb.

secrets.detail_content: In the supporting information box I started to write about how, after my partner Rachel Moore had a breakdown at work in February 2023 and was signed off by her GP at the Orchard Medical Practice in Mansfield for severe anxiety and depression, I shifted the Green Hearth Bistro to a four‑day service model for 12 weeks (from 2023‑03‑06 to 2023‑05‑28) so I could be at home with her in the evenings, using the quieter Mondays to pilot our Community Table lunches and trial a new low‑waste prep schedule that has since become our standard.
secrets.why_sensitive: This discloses Rachel’s specific mental health diagnosis, treatment context, and time off work, which is her private medical information and not necessary for award judges to evaluate Emma’s contribution. It could invite bias or pity and exposes identifiable health details about a third party without consent.

names: Rachel Moore – partner and community mental health nurse
GP at Orchard Medical Practice – treating clinician
events: signed‑off period for severe anxiety and depression

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: Why I’ve cut back the bistro hours for a bit

I’ve told the team we’re going to run Wed–Sat only from 6 March to end of May so I can be home with Rach while she’s signed off for the anxiety and depression. I’m using Mondays to test the Community Table idea so it doesn’t just feel like everything’s on pause.

Event: Reduced opening hours for Rach’s sick leave & Community Table pilot
When: 6 Mar–28 May 2023
Location: Green Hearth Bistro / home

secrets.detail_content: In explaining our community impact, I mentioned that after my dad, Peter Cartwright, was diagnosed with early‑stage bowel cancer at Bassetlaw Hospital in October 2022, I started dedicating one Friday lunch service a month (from 2022‑11‑11 onwards) to a ‘Quiet Table’ scheme for people coming out of chemotherapy, offering a pay‑what‑you‑can menu and discreetly comping around £60–£80 per sitting from the bistro’s profits.
secrets.why_sensitive: This reveals a close family member’s specific cancer diagnosis, treatment status, and hospital, which is highly sensitive health information about someone else. While it shows Emma’s motivation for a community initiative, judges don’t need these intimate medical details to assess her work, and disclosure could violate her father’s privacy and dignity.

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: Dad, the bistro and a new idea

Since Dad’s bowel cancer diagnosis at Bassetlaw in October, I’ve been thinking a lot about how grim those chemo days are. From 11 Nov I’m going to run a monthly ‘Quiet Table’ Friday lunch for people after treatment, pay‑what‑you‑can, and I’ll cover about £60–£80 each time from the bistro.
Notebook entry 2022-10-29: ‘Use Dad’s diagnosis as a push to do something gentle but practical – monthly Quiet Table lunches for chemo patients, keep it low‑key and respectful.’

secrets.detail_content: To show resilience, I drafted a paragraph about how in March 2021 I had to negotiate a time‑to‑pay arrangement with HMRC after falling £7,400 behind on PAYE and VAT for the Green Hearth Bistro during lockdown, and how agreeing to a 12‑month repayment plan starting 2021‑04‑01 forced me to implement the strict weekly waste‑weighing system and menu engineering that ultimately cut our food waste from 42kg to 18kg per week by early 2022.
secrets.why_sensitive: Specific tax arrears amounts, dates, and a formal payment plan with HMRC expose detailed financial vulnerability and potential perceived mismanagement. Award judges don’t need to know exact debts or that HMRC was involved; it could create bias about Emma’s reliability or trigger concerns about ongoing financial risk, even though the situation was responsibly addressed.

names: HMRC case officer (unnamed but implied official role)

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: Confirmed HMRC time to pay

HMRC have agreed to spread the £7,400 PAYE/VAT arrears over 12 months from 1 April 2021. I’m tightening the menu and starting a strict waste‑weighing system to make sure we can meet the payments without cutting staff hours.
Journal 2021-03-18: ‘Scared about HMRC arrears but this is the line in the sand. Use this to justify the waste‑tracking and tough menu changes.’

secrets.detail_content: In the supporting information I was tempted to describe how, after a serious falling‑out with my brother Jonathan Cartwright over our grandmother Mags’ will in August 2020 at my parents’ house in Carlton in Lindrick, I channelled that stress into launching the North Notts Community Kitchen Network in early 2021, emailing four local venues on 2021‑01‑10 to propose a shared surplus‑food scheme so that at least some good would come out of the family row about ‘wasting’ her smallholding legacy.
secrets.why_sensitive: This brings in detailed family conflict around inheritance, including names, dates, and the emotional context. While it explains Emma’s motivation for community work, judges don’t need to know about private disputes over a will; sharing it could damage family relationships and cause others to see Emma or her brother through a lens of personal drama rather than professional merit.

places: parents’ house in Carlton in Lindrick
events: launch of North Notts Community Kitchen Network
initial outreach email to four local venues

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: Turning the mess with Jon into something better

After that awful row over Gran’s will at Mum and Dad’s in August, I’ve decided to put the energy somewhere useful. I’ve emailed four local cafés/pubs today (10 Jan) to see if they’ll join a Community Kitchen Network using surplus food. If the family can’t agree on her land, at least we can honour her by feeding people.
I hate that Jon and I are barely speaking over Gran’s will, but I’m setting up a community kitchen thing in her name. Feels better than just stewing on it.

secrets.detail_content: To illustrate my commitment to staff welfare, I drafted a line about how, after a 19‑year‑old kitchen porter disclosed to me in confidence in May 2024 that he was self‑harming and on a waiting list for counselling through the Bassetlaw Community Mental Health Team, I rearranged the rota at the Green Hearth Bistro from 2024‑05‑13 so he never worked past 9:30pm, personally covered two of his weekend shifts, and built a new check‑in system for all junior staff.
secrets.why_sensitive: This exposes a specific young employee’s mental health struggles and self‑harm, tied to a small workplace and time frame that could make him identifiable. Even though it shows Emma’s care as an employer, sharing this in an awards form violates the staff member’s confidentiality and could have serious repercussions for his privacy and career.

places: Bassetlaw Community Mental Health Team clinic
events: rota changes and new check‑in system for junior staff

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: Worried about J. in the kitchen

J. told me today he’s been self‑harming and is on the Bassetlaw CMHT waiting list. From next week (13 May) I’ve changed the rota so he’s never on past 9:30 and I’m taking two of his weekend shifts. I’m also going to build in regular check‑ins for all the juniors.
Staff notes 2024-05-10: ‘Safeguarding – J. disclosed self‑harm. Keep this confidential, adjust rota, speak to Rach about best way to support without overstepping.’

field_coverage.reasoning: The persona artifact explicitly lists 'Name: Emma Louise Cartwright' and multiple emails are signed 'Emma' or 'Emma Louise Cartwright'. The context of Emma being the subject of the application is clear.
The persona artifact lists 'Employer: The Green Hearth Bistro' and multiple emails reference Emma as owner/head chef at The Green Hearth Bistro.
The persona artifact lists 'Occupation: Owner & Head Chef'. Multiple emails and notes reference Emma as owner and head chef.

field_coverage.field_label: Length Of Employment In Above Position

No artifact contains a landline or telephone number for Emma or The Green Hearth Bistro.
No artifact contains a mobile number for Emma.
The persona artifact lists 'Email: emma.cartwright@greenhearthbistro.co.uk', and Emma uses this email in her signature in at least one email.
No artifact contains the website URL for The Green Hearth Bistro.

field_coverage.field_label: Social Media Links Facebook Instagram Twitter Trip Advisor
field_coverage.reasoning: No artifact contains any social media links or usernames for The Green Hearth Bistro.

Emails are signed 'Emma' or 'Emma Louise Cartwright', which is a natural signature in context. The full 'Emma L. Cartwright' signature is not present, but 'Emma Louise Cartwright' is used in formal email sign-offs.
The persona artifact lists 'Name: Emma Louise Cartwright', and the signature block in emails uses 'Emma Louise Cartwright'.
No artifact contains the specific date '2026-01-22' or any explicit authorisation date.

The content of Box 1 is covered in aggregate by the persona, emails, and notes, which describe Emma's ethos, history, community work, and the bistro's impact in detail, matching the narrative required.
The supporting information (qualifications, awards, community work, etc.) is covered across the persona, notes, and emails, which detail Emma's training, recognitions, and community initiatives.
